parameters.name: x, y
parameters.description: floating point values

fminl.return_value_description: If successful, returns the smaller of two floating point values. The value returned is exact and does not depend on any rounding modes.

fmod.description: The floating-point remainder of the division operation x/y calculated by this function is exactly the value x - n*y, where n is x/y with its fractional part truncated. The returned value has the same sign as x and is less or equal to y in magnitude.

parameters.name: x, y
parameters.description: floating point values

fmod.return_value_description: If successful, returns the floating-point remainder of the division x/y as defined above. If a domain error occurs, an implementation-defined value is returned (NaN where supported). If a range error occurs due to underflow, the correct result (after rounding) is returned.

fmodf.description: The floating-point remainder of the division operation x/y calculated by this function is exactly the value x - n*y, where n is x/y with its fractional part truncated. The returned value has the same sign as x and is less or equal to y in magnitude.

parameters.name: x, y
parameters.description: floating point values

fmodf.return_value_description: If successful, returns the floating-point remainder of the division x/y as defined above. If a domain error occurs, an implementation-defined value is returned (NaN where supported). If a range error occurs due to underflow, the correct result (after rounding) is returned.

fmodl.description: The floating-point remainder of the division operation x/y calculated by this function is exactly the value x - n*y, where n is x/y with its fractional part truncated. The returned value has the same sign as x and is less or equal to y in magnitude.

parameters.name: x, y
parameters.description: floating point values

fmodl.return_value_description: If successful, returns the floating-point remainder of the division x/y as defined above. If a domain error occurs, an implementation-defined value is returned (NaN where supported). If a range error occurs due to underflow, the correct result (after rounding) is returned.

fopen.description: The format of filename is implementation-defined, and does not necessarily refer to a file (e.g. it may be the console or another device accessible though filesystem API). On platforms that support them, filename may include absolute or relative filesystem path.

parameters.name: filename
parameters.description: file name to associate the file stream to

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description: null-terminated character string determining file access mode

parameters.name: streamptr
parameters.description: pointer to a pointer where the function stores the result (an out-parameter)

fopen_s.description: The format of filename is implementation-defined, and does not necessarily refer to a file (e.g. it may be the console or another device accessible though filesystem API). On platforms that support them, filename may include absolute or relative filesystem path.

parameters.name: filename
parameters.description: file name to associate the file stream to

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description: null-terminated character string determining file access mode

parameters.name: streamptr
parameters.description: pointer to a pointer where the function stores the result (an out-parameter)

fpclassify.description: Categorizes floating point value arg into the following categories: zero, subnormal, normal, infinite, NAN, or implementation-defined category. The macro returns an integral value. FLT_EVAL_METHOD is ignored: even if the argument is evaluated with more range and precision than its type, it is first converted to its semantic type, and the classification is based on that: a normal long double value might become subnormal when converted to double and zero when converted to float.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: floating point value

fpclassify.return_value_description: One of FP_INFINITE, FP_NAN, FP_NORMAL, FP_SUBNORMAL, FP_ZERO or implementation-defined type, specifying the category of arg.

fprintf.description: Loads the data from the given locations, converts them to character string equivalents and writes the results to a variety of sinks/streams:

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: output file stream to write to

parameters.name: buffer
parameters.description: pointer to a character string to write to

parameters.name: bufsz
parameters.description: up to bufsz - 1 characters may be written, plus the null terminator

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: pointer to a null-terminated byte string specifying how to interpret the data

arguments specifying data to print. If any argument after default argument promotions is not the type expected by the corresponding conversion specifier, or if there are fewer arguments than required by format, the behavior is undefined. If there are more arguments than required by format, the extraneous arguments are evaluated and ignored.

fprintf_s.description: Loads the data from the given locations, converts them to character string equivalents and writes the results to a variety of sinks/streams:

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: output file stream to write to

parameters.name: buffer
parameters.description: pointer to a character string to write to

parameters.name: bufsz
parameters.description: up to bufsz - 1 characters may be written, plus the null terminator

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: pointer to a null-terminated byte string specifying how to interpret the data

arguments specifying data to print. If any argument after default argument promotions is not the type expected by the corresponding conversion specifier, or if there are fewer arguments than required by format, the behavior is undefined. If there are more arguments than required by format, the extraneous arguments are evaluated and ignored.

fputc.description: Writes a character ch to the given output stream stream. putc() may be implemented as a macro and evaluate stream more than once, so the corresponding argument should never be an expression with side effects. Internally, the character is converted to unsigned char just before being written.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: character to be written

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: output stream

fputc.return_value_description: On success, returns the written character. On failure, returns EOF and sets the error indicator (see ferror()) on stream.

fputs.description: Writes every character from the null-terminated string str to the output stream stream, as if by repeatedly executing fputc. The terminating null character from str is not written.

parameters.name: str
parameters.description: null-terminated character string to be written

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: output stream

fputs.return_value_description: On success, returns a non-negative value On failure, returns EOF and sets the error indicator (see ferror()) on stream.

fputwc.description: Writes a wide character ch to the given output stream stream.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: wide character to be written

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: the output stream

fputwc.return_value_description: Returns a copy of ch on success. On failure, returns WEOF and sets the error indicator (see ferror()) on stream. If an encoding error occurred, additionally sets errno to EILSEQ.

fputws.description: Writes every character from the null-terminated wide string str to the output stream stream, as if by repeatedly executing fputwc. The terminating null wide character from str is not written.

parameters.name: str
parameters.description: null-terminated wide string to be written

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: output stream

fputws.return_value_description: On success, returns a non-negative value On failure, returns EOF and sets the error indicator (see ferror) on stream.

fread.description: Reads up to count objects into the array buffer from the given input stream stream as if by calling fgetc size times for each object, and storing the results, in the order obtained, into the successive positions of buffer, which is reinterpreted as an array of unsigned char. The file position indicator for the stream is advanced by the number of characters read. If an error occurs, the resulting value of the file position indicator for the stream is indeterminate. If a partial element is read, its value is indeterminate.

parameters.name: buffer
parameters.description: pointer to the array where the read objects are stored

parameters.name: size
parameters.description: size of each object in bytes

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: the number of the objects to be read

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: the stream to read

fread.return_value_description: Number of objects read successfully, which may be less than count if an error or end-of-file condition occurs. If size or count is zero, fread returns zero and performs no other action. fread does not distinguish between end-of-file and error, and callers must use feof and ferror to determine which occurred.

free.description: Deallocates the space previously allocated by malloc(), calloc(), aligned_alloc(), (since C11) or realloc(). If ptr is a null pointer, the function does nothing. The behavior is undefined if the value of ptr does not equal a value returned earlier by malloc(), calloc(), realloc(), or aligned_alloc() (since C11). The behavior is undefined if the memory area referred to by ptr has already been deallocated, that is, free(), free_sized(), free_aligned_sized() (since C23), or realloc() has already been called with ptr as the argument and no calls to malloc(), calloc(), realloc(), or aligned_alloc() (since C11) resulted in a pointer equal to ptr afterwards. The behavior is undefined if after free() returns, an access is made through the pointer ptr (unless another allocation function happened to result in a pointer value equal to ptr).

parameters.name: ptr
parameters.description: pointer to the memory to deallocate

free_aligned_sized.description: If ptr is a null pointer or the result obtained from a call to aligned_alloc, where alignment is equal to the requested allocation alignment and size is equal to the requested allocation size, this function is equivalent to free ( ptr). Otherwise, the behavior is undefined. Note: The result of an malloc, calloc, or realloc call may not be passed to free_aligned_sized.

parameters.name: ptr
parameters.description: pointer to the memory to deallocate

parameters.name: alignment
parameters.description: alignment of memory to deallocate

parameters.name: size
parameters.description: size of memory to deallocate

free_sized.description: Deallocates the space previously allocated by malloc(), calloc(), or realloc() (but not aligned_alloc()).

parameters.name: ptr
parameters.description: pointer to the memory to deallocate

parameters.name: size
parameters.description: size of memory previously passed to an allocation function

freopen.description: freopen is the only way to change the narrow/wide orientation of a stream once it has been established by an I/O operation or by fwide. Microsoft CRT version of freopen does not support any mode changes when filename is a null pointer and treats this as an error (see documentation). A possible workaround is the non-standard function _setmode().

parameters.name: filename
parameters.description: file name to associate the file stream to

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description: null-terminated character string determining new file access mode

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: the file stream to modify

parameters.name: newstreamptr
parameters.description: pointer to a pointer where the function stores the result (an out-parameter)

freopen_s.description: freopen is the only way to change the narrow/wide orientation of a stream once it has been established by an I/O operation or by fwide. Microsoft CRT version of freopen does not support any mode changes when filename is a null pointer and treats this as an error (see documentation). A possible workaround is the non-standard function _setmode().

parameters.name: filename
parameters.description: file name to associate the file stream to

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description: null-terminated character string determining new file access mode

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: the file stream to modify

parameters.name: newstreamptr
parameters.description: pointer to a pointer where the function stores the result (an out-parameter)

frexp.description: If arg is zero, returns zero and stores zero in *exp. Otherwise (if arg is not zero), if no errors occur, returns the value x in the range (-1;-0.5], [0.5; 1) and stores an integer value in * exp such that x×2 (*exp) =arg. If the value to be stored in *exp is outside the range of int, the behavior is unspecified. If arg is not a floating-point number, the behavior is unspecified.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: floating point value

parameters.name: exp
parameters.description: pointer to integer value to store the exponent to

frexp.return_value_description: If arg is zero, returns zero and stores zero in *exp. Otherwise (if arg is not zero), if no errors occur, returns the value x in the range (-1;-0.5], [0.5; 1) and stores an integer value in * exp such that x×2 (*exp) =arg. If the value to be stored in *exp is outside the range of int, the behavior is unspecified. If arg is not a floating-point number, the behavior is unspecified.

frexpf.description: If arg is zero, returns zero and stores zero in *exp. Otherwise (if arg is not zero), if no errors occur, returns the value x in the range (-1;-0.5], [0.5; 1) and stores an integer value in * exp such that x×2 (*exp) =arg. If the value to be stored in *exp is outside the range of int, the behavior is unspecified. If arg is not a floating-point number, the behavior is unspecified.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: floating point value

parameters.name: exp
parameters.description: pointer to integer value to store the exponent to

frexpf.return_value_description: If arg is zero, returns zero and stores zero in *exp. Otherwise (if arg is not zero), if no errors occur, returns the value x in the range (-1;-0.5], [0.5; 1) and stores an integer value in * exp such that x×2 (*exp) =arg. If the value to be stored in *exp is outside the range of int, the behavior is unspecified. If arg is not a floating-point number, the behavior is unspecified.

frexpl.description: If arg is zero, returns zero and stores zero in *exp. Otherwise (if arg is not zero), if no errors occur, returns the value x in the range (-1;-0.5], [0.5; 1) and stores an integer value in * exp such that x×2 (*exp) =arg. If the value to be stored in *exp is outside the range of int, the behavior is unspecified. If arg is not a floating-point number, the behavior is unspecified.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: floating point value

parameters.name: exp
parameters.description: pointer to integer value to store the exponent to

frexpl.return_value_description: If arg is zero, returns zero and stores zero in *exp. Otherwise (if arg is not zero), if no errors occur, returns the value x in the range (-1;-0.5], [0.5; 1) and stores an integer value in * exp such that x×2 (*exp) =arg. If the value to be stored in *exp is outside the range of int, the behavior is unspecified. If arg is not a floating-point number, the behavior is unspecified.

fscanf.description: Reads data from a variety of sources, interprets it according to format and stores the results into given locations.

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: input file stream to read from

parameters.name: buffer
parameters.description: pointer to a null-terminated character string to read from

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: pointer to a null-terminated character string specifying how to read the input
receiving arguments.

fscanf_s.description: Reads data from a variety of sources, interprets it according to format and stores the results into given locations.

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: input file stream to read from

parameters.name: buffer
parameters.description: pointer to a null-terminated character string to read from

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: pointer to a null-terminated character string specifying how to read the input
receiving arguments.

fseek.description: Sets the file position indicator for the file stream stream to the value pointed to by offset. If the stream is open in binary mode, the new position is exactly offset bytes measured from the beginning of the file if origin is SEEK_SET, from the current file position if origin is SEEK_CUR, and from the end of the file if origin is SEEK_END. Binary streams are not required to support SEEK_END, in particular if additional null bytes are output. If the stream is open in text mode, the only supported values for offset are zero (which works with any origin) and a value returned by an earlier call to ftell on a stream associated with the same file (which only works with origin of SEEK_SET). If the stream is wide-oriented, the restrictions of both text and binary streams apply (result of ftell is allowed with SEEK_SET and zero offset is allowed from SEEK_SET and SEEK_CUR, but not SEEK_END). In addition to changing the file position indicator, fseek undoes the effects of ungetc and clears the end-of-file status, if applicable. If a read or write error occurs, the error indicator for the stream ( ferror) is set and the file position is unaffected.

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: file stream to modify

parameters.name: offset
parameters.description: number of characters to shift the position relative to origin

parameters.name: origin
parameters.description: position to which offset is added. It can have one of the following values: SEEK_SET, SEEK_CUR, SEEK_END

fsetpos.description: Sets the file position indicator and the multibyte parsing state (if any) for the file stream stream according to the value pointed to by pos. Besides establishing new parse state and position, a call to this function undoes the effects of ungetc and clears the end-of-file state, if it is set. If a read or write error occurs, the error indicator ( ferror) for the stream is set.

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: file stream to modify

parameters.name: pos
parameters.description: pointer to a fpos_t object to use as new value of file position indicator

ftell.description: Returns the file position indicator for the file stream stream. If the stream is open in binary mode, the value obtained by this function is the number of bytes from the beginning of the file. If the stream is open in text mode, the value returned by this function is unspecified and is only meaningful as the input to fseek().

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: file stream to examine

ftell.return_value_description: File position indicator on success or -1L if failure occurs. On error, the errno variable is set to implementation-defined positive value.

fwide.description: If mode > 0, attempts to make stream wide-oriented. If mode < 0, attempts to make stream byte-oriented. If mode==0, only queries the current orientation of the stream. If the orientation of the stream has already been decided (by executing output or by an earlier call to fwide), this function does nothing.

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: pointer to the C I/O stream to modify or query

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description: integer value greater than zero to set the stream wide, less than zero to set the stream narrow, or zero to query only

fwide.return_value_description: An integer greater than zero if the stream is wide-oriented after this call, less than zero if the stream is byte-oriented after this call, and zero if the stream has no orientation.

fwprintf.description: Loads the data from the given locations, converts them to wide string equivalents and writes the results to a variety of sinks.

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: output file stream to write to

parameters.name: buffer
parameters.description: pointer to a wide character string to write to

parameters.name: bufsz
parameters.description: up to bufsz-1 wide characters may be written, plus the null terminator

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: pointer to a null-terminated wide string specifying how to interpret the data

arguments specifying data to print. If any argument after default argument promotions is not the type expected by the corresponding conversion specifier, or if there are fewer arguments than required by format, the behavior is undefined. If there are more arguments than required by format, the extraneous arguments are evaluated and ignored.

fwprintf_s.description: Loads the data from the given locations, converts them to wide string equivalents and writes the results to a variety of sinks.

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: output file stream to write to

parameters.name: buffer
parameters.description: pointer to a wide character string to write to

parameters.name: bufsz
parameters.description: up to bufsz-1 wide characters may be written, plus the null terminator

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: pointer to a null-terminated wide string specifying how to interpret the data

arguments specifying data to print. If any argument after default argument promotions is not the type expected by the corresponding conversion specifier, or if there are fewer arguments than required by format, the behavior is undefined. If there are more arguments than required by format, the extraneous arguments are evaluated and ignored.

fwrite.description: Writes count of objects from the given array buffer to the output stream stream. The objects are written as if by reinterpreting each object as an array of unsigned char and calling fputc size times for each object to write those unsigned char s into stream, in order. The file position indicator for the stream is advanced by the number of characters written. If an error occurs, the resulting value of the file position indicator for the stream is indeterminate.

parameters.name: buffer
parameters.description: pointer to the first object in the array to be written

parameters.name: size
parameters.description: size of each object

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: the number of the objects to be written

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: pointer to the output stream

fwrite.return_value_description: The number of objects written successfully, which may be less than count if an error occurs. If size or count is zero, fwrite returns zero and performs no other action.

fwscanf.description: Reads data from the a variety of sources, interprets it according to format and stores the results into given locations.

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: input file stream to read from

parameters.name: buffer
parameters.description: pointer to a null-terminated wide string to read from

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: pointer to a null-terminated wide string specifying how to read the input
receiving arguments.

fwscanf_s.description: Reads data from the a variety of sources, interprets it according to format and stores the results into given locations.

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: input file stream to read from

parameters.name: buffer
parameters.description: pointer to a null-terminated wide string to read from

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: pointer to a null-terminated wide string specifying how to read the input
receiving arguments.

getc.description: On success, returns the obtained character as an unsigned char converted to an int. On failure, returns EOF. If the failure has been caused by end-of-file condition, additionally sets the eof indicator (see feof()) on stream. If the failure has been caused by some other error, sets the error indicator (see ferror()) on stream.

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: to read the character from

getc.return_value_description: On success, returns the obtained character as an unsigned char converted to an int. On failure, returns EOF. If the failure has been caused by end-of-file condition, additionally sets the eof indicator (see feof()) on stream. If the failure has been caused by some other error, sets the error indicator (see ferror()) on stream.

getchar.description: Reads the next character from stdin. Equivalent to getc ( stdin).
getchar.return_value_description: The obtained character on success or EOF on failure. If the failure has been caused by end-of-file condition, additionally sets the eof indicator (see feof()) on stdin. If the failure has been caused by some other error, sets the error indicator (see ferror()) on stdin.

getenv.description: On POSIX systems, the environment variables are also accessible through the global variable environ, declared as extern char **environ; in <unistd.h>, and through the optional third argument, envp, of the main function. The call to getenv_s with a null pointer for value and zero for valuesz is used to determine the size of the buffer required to hold the entire result.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: null-terminated character string identifying the name of the environmental variable to look for

parameters.name: len
parameters.description: pointer to a user-provided location where getenv_s will store the length of the environment variable

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: pointer to a user-provided character array where getenv_s will store the contents of the environment variable

parameters.name: valuesz
parameters.description: maximum number of characters that getenv_s is allowed to write to dest (size of the buffer)

getenv_s.description: On POSIX systems, the environment variables are also accessible through the global variable environ, declared as extern char **environ; in <unistd.h>, and through the optional third argument, envp, of the main function. The call to getenv_s with a null pointer for value and zero for valuesz is used to determine the size of the buffer required to hold the entire result.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: null-terminated character string identifying the name of the environmental variable to look for

parameters.name: len
parameters.description: pointer to a user-provided location where getenv_s will store the length of the environment variable

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: pointer to a user-provided character array where getenv_s will store the contents of the environment variable

parameters.name: valuesz
parameters.description: maximum number of characters that getenv_s is allowed to write to dest (size of the buffer)

gets.description: str on success, a null pointer on failure. If the failure has been caused by end of file condition, additionally sets the eof indicator (see feof()) on stdin. If the failure has been caused by some other error, sets the error indicator (see ferror()) on stdin.

parameters.name: str
parameters.description: character string to be written

gets.return_value_description: str on success, a null pointer on failure. If the failure has been caused by end of file condition, additionally sets the eof indicator (see feof()) on stdin. If the failure has been caused by some other error, sets the error indicator (see ferror()) on stdin.

gets_s.description: str on success, a null pointer on failure. If the failure has been caused by end of file condition, additionally sets the eof indicator (see feof()) on stdin. If the failure has been caused by some other error, sets the error indicator (see ferror()) on stdin.

parameters.name: str
parameters.description: character string to be written

gets_s.return_value_description: str on success, a null pointer on failure. If the failure has been caused by end of file condition, additionally sets the eof indicator (see feof()) on stdin. If the failure has been caused by some other error, sets the error indicator (see ferror()) on stdin.

getwc.description: Reads the next wide character from the given input stream. getwc() may be implemented as a macro and may evaluate stream more than once.

parameters.name: stream
parameters.description: to read the wide character from

getwc.return_value_description: The next wide character from the stream or WEOF on failure. If the failure has been caused by end-of-file condition, additionally sets the eof indicator (see feof()) on stream. If the failure has been caused by some other error, sets the error indicator (see ferror()) on stream. If an encoding error occurred, additionally sets errno to EILSEQ.

getwchar.description: Reads the next wide character from stdin.
getwchar.return_value_description: the obtained wide character or WEOF if an error has occurred or the end of file reached

gmtime.description: gmtime may not be thread-safe. POSIX requires that gmtime and gmtime_r set errno to EOVERFLOW if they fail because the argument is too large. The implementation of gmtime_s in Microsoft CRT is incompatible with the C standard since it has reversed parameter order.

parameters.name: timer
parameters.description: pointer to a time_t object to convert

parameters.name: buf
parameters.description: pointer to a struct tm object to store the result

gmtime_r.description: gmtime may not be thread-safe. POSIX requires that gmtime and gmtime_r set errno to EOVERFLOW if they fail because the argument is too large. The implementation of gmtime_s in Microsoft CRT is incompatible with the C standard since it has reversed parameter order.

parameters.name: timer
parameters.description: pointer to a time_t object to convert

parameters.name: buf
parameters.description: pointer to a struct tm object to store the result

gmtime_s.description: gmtime may not be thread-safe. POSIX requires that gmtime and gmtime_r set errno to EOVERFLOW if they fail because the argument is too large. The implementation of gmtime_s in Microsoft CRT is incompatible with the C standard since it has reversed parameter order.

parameters.name: timer
parameters.description: pointer to a time_t object to convert

parameters.name: buf
parameters.description: pointer to a struct tm object to store the result

hypot.description: The value computed by this function is the length of the hypotenuse of a right-angled triangle with sides of length x and y, or the distance of the point (x,y) from the origin (0,0), or the magnitude of a complex number x+ i y.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: floating point value

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: floating point value

hypot.return_value_description: If no errors occur, the hypotenuse of a right-angled triangle, \(\scriptsize{\sqrt{x^2+y^2} }\) √ x 2 +y 2, is returned. If a range error due to overflow occurs, +HUGE_VAL, +HUGE_VALF, or +HUGE_VALL is returned. If a range error due to underflow occurs, the correct result (after rounding) is returned.

hypotf.description: The value computed by this function is the length of the hypotenuse of a right-angled triangle with sides of length x and y, or the distance of the point (x,y) from the origin (0,0), or the magnitude of a complex number x+ i y.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: floating point value

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: floating point value

hypotf.return_value_description: If no errors occur, the hypotenuse of a right-angled triangle, \(\scriptsize{\sqrt{x^2+y^2} }\) √ x 2 +y 2, is returned. If a range error due to overflow occurs, +HUGE_VAL, +HUGE_VALF, or +HUGE_VALL is returned. If a range error due to underflow occurs, the correct result (after rounding) is returned.

hypotl.description: The value computed by this function is the length of the hypotenuse of a right-angled triangle with sides of length x and y, or the distance of the point (x,y) from the origin (0,0), or the magnitude of a complex number x+ i y.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: floating point value

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: floating point value

hypotl.return_value_description: If no errors occur, the hypotenuse of a right-angled triangle, \(\scriptsize{\sqrt{x^2+y^2} }\) √ x 2 +y 2, is returned. If a range error due to overflow occurs, +HUGE_VAL, +HUGE_VALF, or +HUGE_VALL is returned. If a range error due to underflow occurs, the correct result (after rounding) is returned.

ignore_handler_s.description: The function simply returns to the caller without performing any other action. A pointer to this function can be passed to set_constraint_handler_s to establish a runtime constraints violation handler that does nothing. As with all bounds-checked functions, ignore_handler_s is only guaranteed to be available if __STDC_LIB_EXT1__ is defined by the implementation and if the user defines __STDC_WANT_LIB_EXT1__ to the integer constant 1 before including <stdlib.h>.

parameters.name: msg
parameters.description: pointer to character string that describes the error

parameters.name: ptr
parameters.description: pointer to an implementation-defined object or a null pointer. Examples of implementation-defined objects are objects that give the name of the function that detected the violation and the line number when the violation was detected

parameters.name: error
parameters.description: the error about to be returned by the calling function, if it happens to be one of the functions that return errno_t

ilogb.description: Formally, the unbiased exponent is the integral part of log r |arg| as a signed integral value, for non-zero arg, where r is FLT_RADIX.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: floating point value

ilogb.return_value_description: If no errors occur, the unbiased exponent of arg is returned as a signed int value. If arg is zero, FP_ILOGB0 is returned. If arg is infinite, INT_MAX is returned. If arg is a NaN, FP_ILOGBNAN is returned. If the correct result is greater than INT_MAX or smaller than INT_MIN, the return value is unspecified and a domain error or range error may occur

ilogbf.description: Formally, the unbiased exponent is the integral part of log r |arg| as a signed integral value, for non-zero arg, where r is FLT_RADIX.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: floating point value

ilogbf.return_value_description: If no errors occur, the unbiased exponent of arg is returned as a signed int value. If arg is zero, FP_ILOGB0 is returned. If arg is infinite, INT_MAX is returned. If arg is a NaN, FP_ILOGBNAN is returned. If the correct result is greater than INT_MAX or smaller than INT_MIN, the return value is unspecified and a domain error or range error may occur

ilogbl.description: Formally, the unbiased exponent is the integral part of log r |arg| as a signed integral value, for non-zero arg, where r is FLT_RADIX.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: floating point value

ilogbl.return_value_description: If no errors occur, the unbiased exponent of arg is returned as a signed int value. If arg is zero, FP_ILOGB0 is returned. If arg is infinite, INT_MAX is returned. If arg is a NaN, FP_ILOGBNAN is returned. If the correct result is greater than INT_MAX or smaller than INT_MIN, the return value is unspecified and a domain error or range error may occur

imaxabs.description: Computes the absolute value of an integer number. The behavior is undefined if the result cannot be represented by the return type.

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: integer value

imaxabs.return_value_description: The absolute value of n (i.e. |n|), if it is representable.

imaxdiv.description: Computes both the quotient and the remainder of the division of the numerator x by the denominator y.

parameters.name: x, y
parameters.description: integer values

imaxdiv.return_value_description: If both the remainder and the quotient can be represented as objects of the corresponding type ( int, long, long long, intmax_t, respectively), returns both as an object of type div_t, ldiv_t, lldiv_t, imaxdiv_t defined as follows: If either the remainder or the quotient cannot be represented, the behavior is undefined.

isalnum.description: Checks if the given character is an alphanumeric character as classified by the current C locale. In the default locale, the following characters are alphanumeric: The behavior is undefined if the value of ch is not representable as unsigned char and is not equal to EOF.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: character to classify

isalnum.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the character is an alphanumeric character, 0 otherwise.

isalpha.description: Checks if the given character is an alphabetic character, i.e. either an uppercase letter ( ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ), or a lowercase letter ( abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz). In locales other than "C", an alphabetic character is a character for which isupper() or islower() returns true or any other character considered alphabetic by the locale. In any case, iscntrl(), isdigit(), ispunct() and isspace() will return false for this character. The behavior is undefined if the value of ch is not representable as unsigned char and is not equal to EOF.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: character to classify

isalpha.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the character is an alphabetic character, zero otherwise.

isblank.description: Checks if the given character is a blank character in the current C locale. In the default C locale, only space ( 0x20) and horizontal tab ( 0x09) are classified as blank. The behavior is undefined if the value of ch is not representable as unsigned char and is not equal to EOF.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: character to classify

isblank.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the character is a blank character, zero otherwise.

iscntrl.description: Checks if the given character is a control character, i.e. codes 0x00-0x1F and 0x7F. The behavior is undefined if the value of ch is not representable as unsigned char and is not equal to EOF.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: character to classify

iscntrl.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the character is a control character, zero otherwise.

isdigit.description: Checks if the given character is a numeric character ( 0123456789). The behavior is undefined if the value of ch is not representable as unsigned char and is not equal to EOF.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: character to classify

isdigit.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the character is a numeric character, zero otherwise.

isfinite.description: Determines if the given floating point number arg has finite value i.e. it is normal, subnormal or zero, but not infinite or NaN. The macro returns an integral value. FLT_EVAL_METHOD is ignored: even if the argument is evaluated with more range and precision than its type, it is first converted to its semantic type, and the classification is based on that.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: floating point value

isfinite.return_value_description: Nonzero integral value if arg has finite value, ​0​ otherwise.

isgraph.description: Checks if the given character has a graphical representation, i.e. it is either a number ( 0123456789), an uppercase letter ( ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ), a lowercase letter ( abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz), or a punctuation character (!"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?@[\]^_`{|}~), or any graphical character specific to the current C locale. The behavior is undefined if the value of ch is not representable as unsigned char and is not equal to EOF.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: character to classify

isgraph.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the character has a graphical representation character, zero otherwise.

isgreater.description: Determines if the floating point number x is greater than the floating-point number ( y), without setting floating-point exceptions.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: floating point value

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: floating point value

isgreater.return_value_description: Nonzero integral value if x > y, ​0​ otherwise.

isgreaterequal.description: Determines if the floating point number x is greater than or equal to the floating-point number y, without setting floating-point exceptions.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: floating point value

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: floating point value

isgreaterequal.return_value_description: Nonzero integral value if x >= y, ​0​ otherwise.

isinf.description: Determines if the given floating-point number arg is positive or negative infinity. The macro returns an integral value. FLT_EVAL_METHOD is ignored: even if the argument is evaluated with more range and precision than its type, it is first converted to its semantic type, and the classification is based on that.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: floating-point value

isinf.return_value_description: Nonzero integral value if arg has an infinite value, ​0​ otherwise.

isless.description: Determines if the floating point number x is less than the floating-point number y, without setting floating-point exceptions.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: floating point value

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: floating point value

isless.return_value_description: Nonzero integral value if x < y, ​0​ otherwise.

islessequal.description: Determines if the floating point number x is less than or equal to the floating-point number y, without setting floating-point exceptions.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: floating point value

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: floating point value

islessequal.return_value_description: Nonzero integral value if x <= y, ​0​ otherwise.

islessgreater.description: Determines if the floating point number x is less than or greater than the floating-point number y, without setting floating-point exceptions.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: floating point value

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: floating point value

islessgreater.return_value_description: Nonzero integral value if x < y || x > y, ​0​ otherwise.

islower.description: Checks if the given character is classified as a lowercase character according to the current C locale. In the default "C" locale, islower returns true only for the lowercase letters ( abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz). If islower returns true, it is guaranteed that iscntrl, isdigit, ispunct, and isspace return false for the same character in the same C locale. The behavior is undefined if the value of ch is not representable as unsigned char and is not equal to EOF.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: character to classify

islower.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the character is a lowercase letter, zero otherwise.

isnan.description: Determines if the given floating point number arg is a not-a-number (NaN) value. The macro returns an integral value. FLT_EVAL_METHOD is ignored: even if the argument is evaluated with more range and precision than its type, it is first converted to its semantic type, and the classification is based on that (this matters if the evaluation type supports NaNs, while the semantic type does not).

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: floating point value

isnan.return_value_description: Nonzero integral value if arg is a NaN, ​0​ otherwise.

isnormal.description: Determines if the given floating point number arg is normal, i.e. is neither zero, subnormal, infinite, nor NaN. The macro returns an integral value. FLT_EVAL_METHOD is ignored: even if the argument is evaluated with more range and precision than its type, it is first converted to its semantic type, and the classification is based on that.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: floating point value

isnormal.return_value_description: Nonzero integral value if arg is normal, ​0​ otherwise.

isprint.description: Checks if the given character can be printed, i.e. it is either a number ( 0123456789), an uppercase letter ( ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ), a lowercase letter ( abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz), a punctuation character(!"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?@[\]^_`{|}~), or space, or any character classified as printable by the current C locale. The behavior is undefined if the value of ch is not representable as unsigned char and is not equal to EOF.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: character to classify

isprint.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the character can be printed, zero otherwise.

ispunct.description: Checks if the given character is a punctuation character in the current C locale. The default C locale classifies the characters!"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?@[\]^_`{|}~ as punctuation. The behavior is undefined if the value of ch is not representable as unsigned char and is not equal to EOF.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: character to classify

ispunct.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the character is a punctuation character, zero otherwise.

isspace.description: Checks if the given character is either The behavior is undefined if the value of ch is not representable as unsigned char and is not equal to EOF.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: character to classify

isspace.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the character is a whitespace character, zero otherwise.

isunordered.description: Determines if the floating point numbers x and y are unordered, that is, one or both are NaN and thus cannot be meaningfully compared with each other.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: floating point value

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: floating point value

isunordered.return_value_description: Nonzero integral value if either x or y is NaN, ​0​ otherwise.

isupper.description: Checks if the given character is an uppercase character according to the current C locale. In the default "C" locale, isupper returns true only for the uppercase letters ( ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ). If isupper returns true, it is guaranteed that iscntrl, isdigit, ispunct, and isspace return false for the same character in the same C locale. The behavior is undefined if the value of ch is not representable as unsigned char and is not equal to EOF.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: character to classify

isupper.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the character is an uppercase letter, zero otherwise.

iswalnum.description: Checks if the given wide character is an alphanumeric character, i.e. either a number ( 0123456789), an uppercase letter ( ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ), a lowercase letter ( abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz) or any alphanumeric character specific to the current locale.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: wide character

iswalnum.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the wide character is an alphanumeric character, zero otherwise.

iswalpha.description: Checks if the given wide character is an alphabetic character, i.e. either an uppercase letter ( ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ), a lowercase letter ( abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz) or any alphabetic character specific to the current locale.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: wide character

iswalpha.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the wide character is an alphabetic character, zero otherwise.

iswblank.description: Checks if the given wide character is classified as blank character (that is, a whitespace character used to separate words in a sentence) by the current C locale. In the default C locale, only space ( 0x20) and horizontal tab ( 0x09) are blank characters.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: wide character

iswblank.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the wide character is a blank character, zero otherwise.

iswcntrl.description: Checks if the given wide character is a control character, i.e. codes 0x00-0x1F and 0x7F and any control characters specific to the current locale.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: wide character

iswcntrl.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the wide character is a control character, zero otherwise.

iswctype.description: Classifies the wide character wc using the current C locale's LC_CTYPE category identified by desc.

parameters.name: wc
parameters.description: the wide character to classify

parameters.name: desc
parameters.description: the LC_CTYPE category, obtained from a call to wctype

iswctype.return_value_description: Non-zero if the character wc has the property identified by desc in LC_CTYPE facet of the current C locale, zero otherwise.

iswdigit.description: Checks if the given wide character corresponds (if narrowed) to one of the ten decimal digit characters 0123456789.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: wide character

iswdigit.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the wide character is a numeric character, zero otherwise.

iswgraph.description: Checks if the given wide character has a graphical representation, i.e. it is either a number ( 0123456789), an uppercase letter ( ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ), a lowercase letter ( abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz), a punctuation character (!"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?@[\]^_`{|}~) or any graphical character specific to the current C locale.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: wide character

iswgraph.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the wide character has a graphical representation character, zero otherwise.

iswlower.description: Checks if the given wide character is a lowercase letter, i.e. one of abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz or any lowercase letter specific to the current locale.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: wide character

iswlower.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the wide character is an lowercase letter, zero otherwise.

iswprint.description: Checks if the given wide character can be printed, i.e. it is either a number ( 0123456789), an uppercase letter ( ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ), a lowercase letter ( abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz), a punctuation character (!"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?@[\]^_`{!}~), space or any printable character specific to the current C locale.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: wide character

iswprint.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the wide character can be printed, zero otherwise.

iswpunct.description: Checks if the given wide character is a punctuation character, i.e. it is one of!"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?@[\]^_`{|}~ or any punctuation character specific to the current locale.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: wide character

iswpunct.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the wide character is a punctuation character, zero otherwise.

iswspace.description: Checks if the given wide character is a whitespace character, i.e. either space ( 0x20), form feed ( 0x0c), line feed ( 0x0a), carriage return ( 0x0d), horizontal tab ( 0x09), vertical tab ( 0x0b) or any whitespace character specific to the current locale.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: wide character

iswspace.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the wide character is a whitespace character, zero otherwise.

iswupper.description: Checks if the given wide character is an uppercase letter, i.e. one of ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ or any uppercase letter specific to the current locale.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: wide character

iswupper.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the wide character is an uppercase letter, zero otherwise.

iswxdigit.description: Checks if the given wide character corresponds (if narrowed) to a hexadecimal numeric character, i.e. one of 0123456789abcdefABCDEF.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: wide character

iswxdigit.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the wide character is a hexadecimal numeric character, zero otherwise.

isxdigit.description: Checks if the given character is a hexadecimal numeric character ( 0123456789 abcdefABCDEF) or is classified as a hexadecimal character. The behavior is undefined if the value of ch is not representable as unsigned char and is not equal to EOF.

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: character to classify

isxdigit.return_value_description: Non-zero value if the character is an hexadecimal numeric character, zero otherwise.

kill_dependency.description: Informs the compiler that the dependency tree started by an memory_order_consume atomic load operation does not extend past the return value of kill_dependency; that is, the argument does not carry a dependency into the return value. The function is implemented as a macro. A is the type of y.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: the expression whose return value is to be removed from a dependency tree

kill_dependency.return_value_description: Returns y, no longer a part of a dependency tree.

localeconv.description: The localeconv function obtains a pointer to a static object of type lconv, which represents numeric and monetary formatting rules of the current C locale.
localeconv.return_value_description: pointer to the current lconv object.

localtime.description: The function localtime may not be thread-safe. POSIX requires that localtime and localtime_r set errno to EOVERFLOW if it fails because the argument is too large. POSIX specifies that the timezone information is determined by localtime and localtime_r as if by calling tzset, which reads the environment variable TZ. The implementation of localtime_s in Microsoft CRT is incompatible with the C standard since it has reversed parameter order and returns errno_t.

parameters.name: timer
parameters.description: pointer to a time_t object to convert

parameters.name: buf
parameters.description: pointer to a struct tm object to store the result

localtime_r.description: The function localtime may not be thread-safe. POSIX requires that localtime and localtime_r set errno to EOVERFLOW if it fails because the argument is too large. POSIX specifies that the timezone information is determined by localtime and localtime_r as if by calling tzset, which reads the environment variable TZ. The implementation of localtime_s in Microsoft CRT is incompatible with the C standard since it has reversed parameter order and returns errno_t.

longjmp.description: Loads the execution context env saved by a previous call to setjmp. This function does not return. Control is transferred to the call site of the macro setjmp that set up env. That setjmp then returns the value, passed as the status. If the function that called setjmp has exited (whether by return or by a different longjmp higher up the stack), the behavior is undefined. In other words, only long jumps up the call stack are allowed.

parameters.name: env
parameters.description: variable referring to the execution state of the program saved by setjmp

parameters.name: status
parameters.description: the value to return from setjmp. If it is equal to ​0​, 1 is used instead

parameters.description: floating point value

lroundf.return_value_description: If no errors occur, the nearest integer value to arg, rounding halfway cases away from zero, is returned. If a domain error occurs, an implementation-defined value is returned.

lroundl.description: If no errors occur, the nearest integer value to arg, rounding halfway cases away from zero, is returned. If a domain error occurs, an implementation-defined value is returned.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: floating point value

lroundl.return_value_description: If no errors occur, the nearest integer value to arg, rounding halfway cases away from zero, is returned. If a domain error occurs, an implementation-defined value is returned.

malloc.description: Allocates size bytes of uninitialized storage. If allocation succeeds, returns a pointer that is suitably aligned for any object type with fundamental alignment. If size is zero, the behavior of malloc is implementation-defined. For example, a null pointer may be returned. Alternatively, a non-null pointer may be returned; but such a pointer should not be dereferenced, and should be passed to free to avoid memory leaks.

parameters.name: size
parameters.description: number of bytes to allocate

malloc.return_value_description: On success, returns the pointer to the beginning of newly allocated memory. To avoid a memory leak, the returned pointer must be deallocated with free() or realloc(). On failure, returns a null pointer.

mblen.description: Determines the size, in bytes, of the multibyte character whose first byte is pointed to by s. If s is a null pointer, resets the global conversion state and (until C23) determined whether shift sequences are used. This function is equivalent to the call mbtowc ( ( wchar_t *) 0, s, n), except that conversion state of mbtowc is unaffected.

parameters.name: s
parameters.description: pointer to the multibyte character

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: limit on the number of bytes in s that can be examined

mblen.return_value_description: If s is not a null pointer, returns the number of bytes that are contained in the multibyte character or -1 if the first bytes pointed to by s do not form a valid multibyte character or ​0​ if s is pointing at the null charcter '\0'. If s is a null pointer, resets its internal conversion state to represent the initial shift state and (until C23) returns ​0​ if the current multibyte encoding is not state-dependent (does not use shift sequences) or a non-zero value if the current multibyte encoding is state-dependent (uses shift sequences).

mbrlen.description: Determines the size, in bytes, of the representation of a multibyte character. This function is equivalent to the call mbrtowc ( NULL, s, n, ps? ps: & internal) for some hidden object internal of type mbstate_t, except that the expression ps is evaluated only once.

parameters.name: s
parameters.description: pointer to an element of a multibyte character string

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: limit on the number of bytes in s that can be examined

parameters.name: ps
parameters.description: pointer to the variable holding the conversion state

mbrlen.return_value_description: The first of the following that applies:

mbrtoc16.description: Converts a single code point from its narrow multibyte character representation to its variable-length 16-bit wide character representation (typically, UTF-16). If s is not a null pointer, inspects at most n bytes of the multibyte character string, beginning with the byte pointed to by s to determine the number of bytes necessary to complete the next multibyte character (including any shift sequences, and taking into account the current multibyte conversion state *ps). If the function determines that the next multibyte character in s is complete and valid, converts it to the corresponding 16-bit wide character and stores it in *pc16 (if pc16 is not null). If the multibyte character in *s corresponds to a multi-char16_t sequence (e.g. a surrogate pair in UTF-16), then after the first call to this function, *ps is updated in such a way that the next call to mbrtoc16 will write out the additional char16_t, without considering *s. If s is a null pointer, the values of n and pc16 are ignored and the call is equivalent to mbrtoc16 ( NULL, "", 1, ps). If the wide character produced is the null character, the conversion state *ps represents the initial shift state. If the macro __STDC_UTF_16__ is defined, the 16-bit encoding used by this function is UTF-16; otherwise, it is implementation-defined. The macro is always defined and the encoding is always UTF-16. (since C23) In any case, the multibyte character encoding used by this function is specified by the currently active C locale.

parameters.name: pc16
parameters.description: pointer to the location where the resulting 16-bit wide character will be written

parameters.name: s
parameters.description: pointer to the multibyte character string used as input

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: limit on the number of bytes in s that can be examined

parameters.name: ps
parameters.description: pointer to the conversion state object used when interpreting the multibyte string

mbrtoc16.return_value_description: The first of the following that applies:

mbrtoc32.description: Converts a single code point from its narrow multibyte character representation to its variable-length 32-bit wide character representation (but typically, UTF-32). If s is not a null pointer, inspects at most n bytes of the multibyte character string, beginning with the byte pointed to by s to determine the number of bytes necessary to complete the next multibyte character (including any shift sequences, and taking into account the current multibyte conversion state *ps). If the function determines that the next multibyte character in s is complete and valid, converts it to the corresponding 32-bit wide character and stores it in *pc32 (if pc32 is not null). If the multibyte character in *s corresponds to a multi-char32_t sequence (not possible with UTF-32), then after the first call to this function, *ps is updated in such a way that the next calls to mbrtoc32 will write out the additional char32_t, without considering *s. If s is a null pointer, the values of n and pc32 are ignored and the call is equivalent to mbrtoc32 ( NULL, "", 1, ps). If the wide character produced is the null character, the conversion state *ps represents the initial shift state. If the macro __STDC_UTF_32__ is defined, the 32-bit encoding used by this function is UTF-32; otherwise, it is implementation-defined. The macro is always defined and the encoding is always UTF-32. (since C23) In any case, the multibyte character encoding used by this function is specified by the currently active C locale.

parameters.name: pc32
parameters.description: pointer to the location where the resulting 32-bit wide character will be written

parameters.name: s
parameters.description: pointer to the multibyte character string used as input

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: limit on the number of bytes in s that can be examined

parameters.name: ps
parameters.description: pointer to the conversion state object used when interpreting the multibyte string

mbrtoc32.return_value_description: The first of the following that applies:

mbrtoc8.description: Converts a narrow multibyte character to UTF-8 encoding. If s is not a null pointer, inspects at most n bytes of the multibyte character string, beginning with the byte pointed to by s to determine the number of bytes necessary to complete the next multibyte character (including any shift sequences). If the function determines that the next multibyte character in s is complete and valid, converts it to UTF-8 and stores the first UTF-8 code unit in *pc8 (if pc8 is not null). If UTF-8 encoding of the multibyte character in *s consists of more than one UTF-8 code unit, then after the first call to this function, *ps is updated in such a way that the next call to mbrtoc8 will write out the additional UTF-8 code units, without considering *s. If s is a null pointer, the values of n and pc8 are ignored and the call is equivalent to mbrtoc8(nullptr, "", 1, ps). If UTF-8 code unit produced is u8'\0', the conversion state *ps represents the initial shift state. The multibyte encoding used by this function is specified by the currently active C locale.

parameters.name: pc8
parameters.description: pointer to the location where the resulting UTF-8 code units will be written

parameters.name: s
parameters.description: pointer to the multibyte character string used as input

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: limit on the number of bytes in s that can be examined

parameters.name: ps
parameters.description: pointer to the conversion state object used when interpreting the multibyte string

mbrtoc8.return_value_description: The first of the following that applies:

mbrtowc.description: Converts a narrow multibyte character to its wide character representation. If s is not a null pointer, inspects at most n bytes of the multibyte character string, beginning with the byte pointed to by s to determine the number of bytes necessary to complete the next multibyte character (including any shift sequences, and taking into account the current multibyte conversion state *ps). If the function determines that the next multibyte character in s is complete and valid, converts it to the corresponding wide character and stores it in *pwc (if pwc is not null). If s is a null pointer, the values of n and pwc are ignored and call is equivalent to mbrtowc ( NULL, "", 1, ps). If the wide character produced is the null character, the conversion state stored in *ps is the initial shift state. If the environment macro __STDC_ISO_10646__ is defined, the values of type wchar_t are the same as the short identifiers of the characters in the Unicode required set (typically UTF-32 encoding); otherwise, it is implementation-defined. In any case, the multibyte character encoding used by this function is specified by the currently active C locale.

parameters.name: pwc
parameters.description: pointer to the location where the resulting wide character will be written

parameters.name: s
parameters.description: pointer to the multibyte character string used as input

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: limit on the number of bytes in s that can be examined

parameters.name: ps
parameters.description: pointer to the conversion state used when interpreting the multibyte character string

mbrtowc.return_value_description: The first of the following that applies:

mbsinit.description: If ps is not a null pointer, the mbsinit function determines whether the pointed-to mbstate_t object describes the initial conversion state.

parameters.name: ps
parameters.description: pointer to the mbstate_t object to examine

mbsinit.return_value_description: ​0​ if ps is not a null pointer and does not represent the initial conversion state, nonzero value otherwise.

parameters.name: dst
parameters.description: pointer to wide character array where the results will be stored

parameters.name: src
parameters.description: pointer to pointer to the first element of a null-terminated multibyte string

parameters.name: len

parameters.description: pointer to the conversion state object

parameters.name: dstsz
parameters.description: max number of wide characters that will be written (size of the dst array)

parameters.name: retval
parameters.description: pointer to a size_t object where the result will be stored

parameters.name: dst
parameters.description: pointer to wide character array where the results will be stored

mbstowcs.description: In most implementations, mbstowcs updates a global static object of type mbstate_t as it processes through the string, and cannot be called simultaneously by two threads, mbsrtowcs should be used in such cases. POSIX specifies a common extension: if dst is a null pointer, this function returns the number of wide characters that would be written to dst, if converted. Similar behavior is standard for mbstowcs_s and for mbsrtowcs.

parameters.name: dst
parameters.description: pointer to wide character array where the wide string will be stored

parameters.name: src
parameters.description: pointer to the first element of a null-terminated multibyte string to convert

parameters.name: len
parameters.description: number of wide characters available in the array pointed to by dst

parameters.name: dstsz
parameters.description: max number of wide characters that will be written (size of the dst array)

parameters.name: retval
parameters.description: pointer to a size_t object where the result will be stored

POSIX specifies a common extension: if dst is a null pointer, this function returns the number of wide characters that would be written to dst, if converted. Similar behavior is standard for mbstowcs_s and for mbsrtowcs.

parameters.name: dst
parameters.description: pointer to wide character array where the wide string will be stored

parameters.name: src
parameters.description: pointer to the first element of a null-terminated multibyte string to convert

parameters.description: max number of wide characters that will be written (size of the dst array)

parameters.description: pointer to a size_t object where the result will be stored

mbtowc.description: Converts a multibyte character whose first byte is pointed to by s to a wide character, written to *pwc if pwc is not null. If s is a null pointer, resets the global conversion state and determines whether shift sequences are used.

parameters.name: pwc
parameters.description: pointer to the wide character for output

parameters.name: s
parameters.description: pointer to the multibyte character

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: limit on the number of bytes in s that can be examined

mbtowc.return_value_description: If s is not a null pointer, returns the number of bytes that are contained in the multibyte character or -1 if the first bytes pointed to by s do not form a valid multibyte character or ​0​ if s is pointing at the null character '\0'. If s is a null pointer, resets its internal conversion state to represent the initial shift state and returns ​0​ if the current multibyte encoding is not state-dependent (does not use shift sequences) or a non-zero value if the current multibyte encoding is state-dependent (uses shift sequences).

memccpy.description: Copies bytes from the object pointed to by src to the object pointed to by dest, stopping after any of the next two conditions are satisfied: The src and dest objects are interpreted as arrays of unsigned char. The behavior is undefined if any condition is met:

parameters.name: dest
parameters.description: pointer to the object to copy to

parameters.name: src
parameters.description: pointer to the object to copy from

parameters.name: c
parameters.description: terminating byte, converted to unsigned char at first

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: number of bytes to copy

memccpy.return_value_description: If the byte (unsigned char)c was found, memccpy returns a pointer to the next byte in dest after (unsigned char)c. Otherwise it returns a null pointer.

memchr.description: The behavior is undefined if access occurs beyond the end of the array searched. The behavior is undefined if ptr is a null pointer.

parameters.name: ptr
parameters.description: pointer to the object to be examined

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: bytes to search for

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: max number of bytes to examine

memchr.return_value_description: Pointer to the location of the byte, or a null pointer if no such byte is found.

memcmp.description: Compares the first count bytes of the objects pointed to by lhs and rhs. The comparison is done lexicographically. The sign of the result is the sign of the difference between the values of the first pair of bytes (both interpreted as unsigned char) that differ in the objects being compared. The behavior is undefined if access occurs beyond the end of either object pointed to by lhs and rhs. The behavior is undefined if either lhs or rhs is a null pointer.

parameters.name: lhs, rhs
parameters.description: pointers to the objects to compare

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: number of bytes to examine

memcmp.return_value_description: Negative value if lhs appears before rhs in lexicographical order. Zero if lhs and rhs compare equal, or if count is zero. Positive value if lhs appears after rhs in lexicographical order.

memcpy.description: memcpy may be used to set the effective type of an object obtained by an allocation function. memcpy is the fastest library routine for memory-to-memory copy. It is usually more efficient than strcpy, which must scan the data it copies or memmove, which must take precautions to handle overlapping inputs. Several C compilers transform suitable memory-copying loops to memcpy calls. Where strict aliasing prohibits examining the same memory as values of two different types, memcpy may be used to convert the values.

parameters.name: dest
parameters.description: pointer to the object to copy to

parameters.name: destsz
parameters.description: max number of bytes to modify in the destination (typically the size of the destination object)

parameters.name: src
parameters.description: pointer to the object to copy from

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: number of bytes to copy

memcpy_s.description: memcpy may be used to set the effective type of an object obtained by an allocation function. memcpy is the fastest library routine for memory-to-memory copy. It is usually more efficient than strcpy, which must scan the data it copies or memmove, which must take precautions to handle overlapping inputs. Several C compilers transform suitable memory-copying loops to memcpy calls. Where strict aliasing prohibits examining the same memory as values of two different types, memcpy may be used to convert the values.

parameters.name: dest
parameters.description: pointer to the object to copy to

parameters.name: destsz
parameters.description: max number of bytes to modify in the destination (typically the size of the destination object)

parameters.name: src
parameters.description: pointer to the object to copy from

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: number of bytes to copy

memmove.description: memmove may be used to set the effective type of an object obtained by an allocation function. Despite being specified "as if" a temporary buffer is used, actual implementations of this function do not incur the overhead or double copying or extra memory. A common approach (glibc and bsd libc) is to copy bytes forwards from the beginning of the buffer if the destination starts before the source, and backwards from the end otherwise, with a fall back to the more efficient memcpy when there is no overlap at all. Where strict aliasing prohibits examining the same memory as values of two different types, memmove may be used to convert the values.

parameters.name: dest
parameters.description: pointer to the object to copy to

parameters.name: destsz
parameters.description: max number of bytes to modify in the destination (typically the size of the destination object)

parameters.name: src
parameters.description: pointer to the object to copy from

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: number of bytes to copy

Despite being specified "as if" a temporary buffer is used, actual implementations of this function do not incur the overhead or double copying or extra memory. A common approach (glibc and bsd libc) is to copy bytes forwards from the beginning of the buffer if the destination starts before the source, and backwards from the end otherwise, with a fall back to the more efficient memcpy when there is no overlap at all. Where strict aliasing prohibits examining the same memory as values of two different types, memmove may be used to convert the values.

parameters.name: dest

parameters.name: count

memset.description: memset may be optimized away (under the as-if rules) if the object modified by this function is not accessed again for the rest of its lifetime (e.g., gcc bug 8537). For that reason, this function cannot be used to scrub memory (e.g., to fill an array that stored a password with zeroes). This optimization is prohibited for memset_explicit and memset_s: they are guaranteed to perform the memory write. Third-party solutions for that include FreeBSD explicit_bzero or Microsoft SecureZeroMemory.

parameters.name: dest
parameters.description: pointer to the object to fill

parameters.name: ch
parameters.description: fill byte

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: number of bytes to fill

parameters.name: destsz
parameters.description: size of the destination array

memset_explicit.description: memset may be optimized away (under the as-if rules) if the object modified by this function is not accessed again for the rest of its lifetime (e.g., gcc bug 8537). For that reason, this function cannot be used to scrub memory (e.g., to fill an array that stored a password with zeroes). This optimization is prohibited for memset_explicit and memset_s: they are guaranteed to perform the memory write. Third-party solutions for that include FreeBSD explicit_bzero or Microsoft SecureZeroMemory.

parameters.name: dest

For that reason, this function cannot be used to scrub memory (e.g., to fill an array that stored a password with zeroes). This optimization is prohibited for memset_explicit and memset_s: they are guaranteed to perform the memory write. mktime.description: Renormalizes local calendar time expressed as a struct tm object and also converts it to time since epoch as a time_t object. arg->tm_wday and arg->tm_yday are ignored. The values in arg are not checked for being out of range. A negative value of arg->tm_isdst causes mktime to attempt to determine if Daylight Saving Time was in effect in the specified time. If the conversion to time_t is successful, the arg object is modified. All fields of arg are updated to fit their proper ranges. arg->tm_wday and arg->tm_yday are recalculated using information available in other fields.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: pointer to a tm object specifying local calendar time to convert

mktime.return_value_description: time since epoch as a time_t object on success, or -1 if arg cannot be represented as a time_t object (POSIX also requires EOVERFLOW to be stored in errno in this case).

modf.description: If no errors occur, returns the fractional part of x with the same sign as x. The integral part is put into the value pointed to by iptr. The sum of the returned value and the value stored in *iptr gives arg (allowing for rounding).

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: floating point value

parameters.name: iptr
parameters.description: pointer to floating point value to store the integral part to

modf.return_value_description: If no errors occur, returns the fractional part of x with the same sign as x. The integral part is put into the value pointed to by iptr. The sum of the returned value and the value stored in *iptr gives arg (allowing for rounding).

modff.description: If no errors occur, returns the fractional part of x with the same sign as x. The integral part is put into the value pointed to by iptr. The sum of the returned value and the value stored in *iptr gives arg (allowing for rounding).

parameters.description: floating point value

parameters.name: iptr
